coredata.dc:title: pH assisted selective detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I) based on nitrogen-rich carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Liang
Wang, Pei
Wang, Yuanhao
Wang, Fu
coredata.dc:description: Novel nitrogen rich carbon dots were synthesized via a hydrothermal method by using with 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine as the precursor. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy showed that the carbon dots contained 41 at.% nitrogen; this is significantly higher than previously reported values for similar materials. When exposed to ultraviolet light, the carbon dots in solution emitted bright blue fluorescence, with a maximum quantum yield of 74.9%. The fluorescence was selectively quenched by Hg(II) at pH 6 and Ag(I) at pH 11, with detection limits of 11.4 and 10.3 nM, respectively. Analysis of the fluorescence lifetimes of the carbon dots showed that the fluorescence-quenching mechanism for Hg(II) differed from that for Ag(I). In analysis of lake water, the nitrogen-rich carbon dots were highly sensitive, and gave a wide linear range and satisfactory recovery. These results suggest that the carbon dots are suitable for use as novel metal ion probes for the selective detection of different ions at different pH values.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
2,4,6-Triaminopyrimidine
Fluorescent probe
Hg(II) and Ag(I) detection
pH assisted selectivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity of TAPI-dots for metal ions
Mechanism of TAPI-dot sensing of Hg(II) and Ag(I)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21503271 ) and Shenzhen Peacock Plan ( KQTD2015071616442225 ) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthesis of TAPI-dots and their use for selective detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I) at different pH values.

(a) TEM and (b) AFM images of TAPI-dots; (c) UV–vis absorption and FL excitation/emission spectra of TAPI-dots; inset shows photographs of TAPI-dots solution when irradiated at 365 nm; and (d) FL spectra of TAPI-dot solution when exposed to different excitation wavelengths between 300 and 380 nm.
(a) XP spectra of TAPI-dots and high-resolution (b) C1s, (c) O1s, and (d) N1s XP spectra.
(a) and (b) Dependence of FL intensity on pH (pH 1–13); (c) FL intensity of TAPI-dots at different NaCl solution concentrations; and (d) photostability of TAPI-dots when excited at 330 nm.
FL intensity of TAPI-dot solution containing Hg(II) or Ag(I) (5 μM) from pH 2 to 13. F and F 0 are PL intensities in the presence and absence, respectively, of metal ions.

(a) FL spectra of TAPI-dots in presence of different concentrations of Hg(II); (b) calibration curve of normalized FL intensity as function of Hg(II) concentration at pH 6; (c) FL spectra of TAPI-dots in presence of different concentrations of Ag(I); and (d) calibration curve of normalized FL intensity as function of Ag(I) concentration at pH 11.

Selectivities of TAPI-dots for different metal ions at pH 6 or 11. Concentrations of Cd(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Cr(VI), Cu(II), Ni(I), Pb(II), Mn(II), Hg(II), and Ag(I) were 5 μM, and those of Al(III), Ca(II), Fe(III), K(I), Mg(II), Na(I), and Zn(II) were 100 μM.
(a) FL decay curves for TAPI-dots in absence and presence of Hg(II) ions (10 μM) at pH 6 and (b) FL decay curves for TAPI-dots in absence and presence of Ag(I) ions (10 μM) at pH 11.
Comparison of multiple-metal detection results for different fluorescent probes.

head.ce:title: pH assisted selective detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I) based on nitrogen-rich carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, 800 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai, 200240, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Institute of Pollution Control and Ecological Security, Shanghai, 200092, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Institute of Pollution Control and Ecological Security

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Environmental Sciences and Technology, Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics & Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Urumqi, Xinjiang, 830011, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Environmental Sciences and Technology
Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics & Chemistry

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, 800 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai, 200240, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel nitrogen rich carbon dots were synthesized via a hydrothermal method by using with 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine as the precursor. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy showed that the carbon dots contained 41 at.% nitrogen; this is significantly higher than previously reported values for similar materials. When exposed to ultraviolet light, the carbon dots in solution emitted bright blue fluorescence, with a maximum quantum yield of 74.9%. The fluorescence was selectively quenched by Hg(II) at pH 6 and Ag(I) at pH 11, with detection limits of 11.4 and 10.3 nM, respectively. Analysis of the fluorescence lifetimes of the carbon dots showed that the fluorescence-quenching mechanism for Hg(II) differed from that for Ag(I). In analysis of lake water, the nitrogen-rich carbon dots were highly sensitive, and gave a wide linear range and satisfactory recovery. These results suggest that the carbon dots are suitable for use as novel metal ion probes for the selective detection of different ions at different pH values.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ion pollution is a significant problem because of accumulation in the environment and potential hazards to human health [1,2]. Therefore, various kinds of approaches have been developed for heavy metal ions analyses [3,4]. Of these different sensing platforms [5–7], fluorescence (FL) based detection is widely used because it is cheap, simple and highly sensitive. A range of fluorescent probes, e.g., based on organic molecules, semiconductor quantum dots, and metal nanoclusters, have been developed and used for the detection of metal ions [8–10].

Among these fluorescent probes, carbon dots (CDs) show great promise for the selective detection of heavy metal ions. Importantly, they are non-toxic, biocompatible and photostable [11,12]. Oxidized CDs respond preferentially to Cu(II) or Fe(III) [13–15] but highly toxic metal ions, including Hg(II), Ag(I), and Pb(II), can be detected with high sensitivity by doping CDs with various elements (N, P, or S) [16–19]. Notably, CDs doped with heteroatoms can be sensitive to different metal ions simultaneously, which allows the development of FL sensing for complex systems.

Ren et al. prepared N doped CDs from uric acid and used them for the detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I) [17] Ye et al. synthesized N and S co-doped CDs from biomass, and that exhibited a highly sensitive and selective response to Hg(II) and Ag(I) [20]. However, the response to individual ions was limited because these systems required a masking agent to protect one ion before detecting the other. For example, Wang et al. reported that the addition of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid or NH4OH enabled selective detection of Hg(II) or Ag(I) ions with carbon nanoribbons [21]. The presence of masking agents causes fluctuations in the FL intensity and interferes with the accuracy of trace level analysis, and complicates the detection procedure. The development of facile and effective methods for detecting multiple metal ions without the need for masking agents is therefore important.

Here, we report a facile synthesis of CDs from 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine (TAPI) via a one-step hydrothermal method; these CDs are referred to as TAPI-dots. These TAPI-dots were used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of either Hg(II) or Ag(I) by simply adjusting the pH of the sample solution (Scheme 1 ). A combination of Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy showed that NH, CN, and CN groups covered the TAPI-dot surface. A FL-quenching mechanism was proposed based on FL lifetime measurements. The selectivity of the TAPI-dots for Hg(II) or Ag(I) depends on whether the dot surface is protonated or deprotonated, and this depends on the solution pH. The nitrogen-rich TAPI-dots performed reliably in lake water tests. This shows that they have potential applications in the selective detection of different metal ions.

All chemicals were analytical grade and used without purification. 2,4,6-Triaminopyrimidine (1004-38-2) and Cd(NO3)2·4H2O were purchased from Alfa-Aesar and Sigma-Aldrich, respectively. AgNO3, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, HNO3, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, NaH2PO4·2H2O Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O, and NaOH were purchased from Tianjin BaiShi. AlCl3·6H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, CaCl2 HgCl2, KNO3, NaNO3, MnCl2, Pb(NO3)2 and ZnCl2 were purchased from Tianjin GuangFu. Deionized water (DI water, ≥18.2 MΩ) was used for the preparation of aqueous solutions. HNO3, phosphate buffer solution (PBS), and NaOH were used to control the solution pH. Cationic standards of required concentrations were obtained by diluting stock solutions with DI water.

The surface morphology of the as-prepared TAPI-dots was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Hitachi, H-7650), and atomic force microscopy (AFM; Bruker Multimode 8). Constituent elements and functional groups were identified by XPS (EDAX, Genesis 60S) and FT-IR spectroscopy (BIO-RAD FTS165). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed at room temperature with a Malvern Zetasizer NanoZS90 instrument. UV–vis spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrometer (Japan). The FL intensity of the test solution was determined with a Hitachi F-7000 luminescence spectrometer (Japan) at an excitation slit width of 5 nm. The FL lifetime was determined with a time-resolved FluoroLog-3 modular spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Jvon, France) at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm.

TAPI (2 mM) was dissolved in water (20 mL). The solution was stirred for 20 min and then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. A yellow, transparent solution was obtained. After centrifugation, the supernatant was collected.

A TAPI-dots solution (5 μL, C TAPI-dots = 2.5 μL mL−1, 0.0312 mg mL−1) was added to a measured amount of metal ions to give a clear solution (2 mL). The pH was adjusted with PBS (1 mM) to pH 6 or with NaOH (1 mM) to pH 11. After 1 min, the mixture was examined by FL spectroscopy (λ ex = 330 nm) at ambient temperature.

The QY of the TAPI-dots in water were calculated using the following equation:
ΦX = ΦST(GrandX/GrandST)(ηX 2/ηST 2)(AST/AX),
where Φ is the QY, Grand is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscript ST refers to a standard (anthracene) with a known QY and X refers to the sample. To minimize re-absorption effects, UV absorption was kept below 0.05 at λ ex = 330 nm.

The morphology of the TAPI-dots was examined using TEM and AFM; the results are shown in Fig. 1 . The TAPI-dots had a narrow size distribution with an average size of 2.7 nm (Fig. 1a). This was confirmed by DLS, as shown in Fig. S1. High-resolution TEM (top inset in Fig. 1a) showed that the TAPI-dots were crystalline with a lattice spacing of approximately 0.251 nm, which is close to the (020) lattice spacing of graphite [22]. AFM showed that the TAPI-dots were of height 0.4–1.2 nm (Fig. 1b); this corresponds to one to two carbon layers [23]. The TEM and AFM analyses confirmed that the TAPI molecules were transformed to CDs after hydrothermal reaction for 12 h.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of a TAPI-dot solution is shown in Fig. 1c. The peaks at 227 and 312 nm were assigned to π–π* sp2 aromatic conjugation and the n–π* C = O transition, respectively [24,25]. On excitation at 330 nm, the maximum emission intensity for the TAPI-dots was observed near 370 nm. This excitation wavelength was therefore used in subsequent experiments. Excitation-dependent emission was observed, with red-shifts of the emission peaks; this is typical of CDs [26,27].

FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS were used to study the surface properties of the TAPI-dots. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S2) showed strong absorption bands characteristic of CN heterocycles. The peaks at 1632 and 1416 cm−1 were assigned to CN and CN stretching, and the band at 3118 cm−1 was assigned to NH stretching [28,29]. The bands at 3400 and 1030–1310 cm−1 suggest the presence of CO groups [30,31]. The presence of N- and O-containing groups on the TAPI-dot surfaces suggests that they should disperse effectively in water.

XPS was used to determine the elemental composition of the TAPI-dots; the results are shown in Fig. 2 . Three peaks were observed in the XP spectrum, at 284.0, 400.0, and 530.6 eV; these correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s transitions [32], respectively (Fig. 2a). The TAPI-dots were therefore primarily composed of C (48.6 at%), N (41.0 at%), and O (10.4 at%). The high-resolution C1s XP spectrum contained three peaks, at 284.8, 287.4, and 288.6 eV, which were attributed to CC, CN, and CO groups, respectively (Fig. 2b) [20,33]. The high-resolution N1s XP spectrum contained peaks at 398.1, 399.5, and 400.4 eV; these were assigned to N (pyridinic-type N), N (pyrrolic-type N), and NH groups, respectively (Fig. 2c) [34]. The high-resolution O1s XP spectrum contained two peaks, at 531.3 and 532.6 eV, which were assigned to CO and CO groups, respectively [35–37]. The XPS and FT-IR data confirm that nitrogen groups were abundant on the TAPI-dot surface.
To the best of our knowledge, these TAPI-dots have the highest reported percentage of N (41.0 at%) in CDs. This may give more binding sites for anchoring specific metal ions [21,38].

The FL intensities of the TAPI-dots in solutions of pH from 1 to 13 were determined; the results are shown in Fig. 3 a. The FL intensity was relatively stable between pH 4 and 8. At pH values lower than 4 or higher than 9, the FL intensity increased, with a maximum value at pH 2 (Fig. 3b). The QY was calculated to be 74.9%.

The FL intensity was also measured in the presence of increasing concentrations of NaCl (up to 0.5 M) to evaluate the stability of the TAPI-dots in highly ionic environments. Only a negligible change was observed in the FL intensity (Fig. 3c). This indicates that the TAPI-dots could be used to analyze highly ionic industrial sewage.

Time-dependent photoluminescence (PL) experiments were performed to determine the photostability of TAPI-dots in solution. Irradiation of a TAPI-dot solution for 2 h (Xe lamp, 150 W, 330 nm) decreased the FL intensity of the solution by 2%. This indicates that the TAPI-dots are sufficiently photostable (Fig. 3d). We also evaluated the effects on the TAPI-dots of storage. After storage for one month at ambient temperature, a TAPI-dot solution remained clear, without any signs of precipitation or obvious changes in the FL behavior (Fig. S3). The ability of the TAPI-dots to work in the presence of high concentrations of ions, their photostability, and their long-term storage capacity means that they have great promise for use in FL-sensing applications.

The ability of the TAPI-dots to detect metal ions effectively was investigated. The FL of the TAPI-dots was quenched in the presence of Hg(II) and Ag(I); this is consistent with the behavior of the majority of N-doped CDs [16,21,37]. Moreover, the responses to Hg(II) and Ag(I) (5 μM) at different pH values were different as shown in Fig. 4 . The FL of the TAPI-dots was quenched selectively by Hg(II) at pH 6 and by Ag(I) at pH 11. Importantly, the presence of both metal ions did not interfere significantly with selective detection. We therefore used pH values of 6 and 11 to investigate the detection of Hg(II) and Ag(I), respectively.

The detection sensitivity of the TAPI-dots was evaluated by determining the FL intensities of test solutions containing different concentrations of Hg(II) and Ag(I); the results are shown in Fig. 5 . The FL intensity at 370 nm decreased gradually with increasing concentrations of Hg(II) and Ag(I). The FL of the TAPI-dots was almost completely quenched in the presence of 10 μM Hg(II), i.e., the sensitivity range for Hg(II) ions was 0–10 μM. The correlation equation used to fit the data is F/F 0 = 0.962 − 0.086C Hg(II) (0–7 μM, R 2 = 0.991), where C is the concentration (μM), and F and F 0 are the PL intensities in the presence and absence, respectively, of Hg(II). The limit of detection (LOD) for Hg(II) was 11.4 nM (evaluated at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3) (Fig. 5b).

The correlation equations for sensing Ag(I) are F/F 0 = 0.995 − 0.136C Ag(I) (0 to 1 μM, R 2 = 0.994) and F/F 0 = 0.922 − 0.076C Ag(I) (1 to 8 μM, R 2 = 0.995), with a LOD for Ag(I) of 10.3 nM from 0 to 14 μM (Fig. 5d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of TAPI-dots for metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to sensitivity, selectivity is important for an effective sensing material. The FL intensities of the TAPI-dots were therefore measured in the presence of 17 different metal ions, namely Cd(II), Co(II), Cr(III), Cr(VI), Cu(II), Ni(I), Pb(II), Al(III), Ca(II), Fe(III), K(I), Mg(II), Mn(II), Na(I), Zn(II), Hg(II), and Ag(I); the results are shown in Fig. 6 . At pH 6, the FL was quenched significantly by Hg(II), whereas the other metal ions did not noticeably affect the FL response of the TAPI-dots. A high selectivity for FL quenching by Ag(I) was also observed, at pH 11. The negligible effects of the other metal ions indicate that the TAPI-dots could be used as a selective metal sensor in practical situations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of TAPI-dot sensing of Hg(II) and Ag(I)
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanisms of Hg(II) and Ag(I) sensing by TAPI-dots were investigated by examining the FL lifetimes before and after quenching; the results are shown in Fig. 7 . The QYs, zeta potentials, and DLS average sizes of the TAPI-dots at different pH values were also determined and compared (Table S1). The FL lifetime of the TAPI-dots decreased significantly after addition of Hg(II) (Fig. 7a) but did not change after addition of Ag(I) (Fig. 7b). A combination of these results and known electron and energy transfer mechanisms [39], suggests that the FL-quenching mechanisms for Hg(II) and Ag(I) are different. The FL lifetimes in the presence of Hg(II) indicate dynamic quenching, whereas those in the presence of Ag(I) indicate static quenching. To further confirm the pH function, different electrolyte solution including NaOH, KOH, Na2HPO3, NH3OH ware also used to control the pH and similar FL quenching resualts were also obtained, suggestting it is the work of OH−, not the electrolyte.

We previously discussed the nitrogen-containing groups on the surfaces of the TAPI-dots. Nitrogen plays an important role in the selectivity of CDs for metal ions [21,38]. At low pH, the surface groups of the TAPI-dots will be largely protonated, which limits the affinity of the CDs for metal ions [40]. At pH less than 4, Hg(II) or Ag(I) therefore could not affect the FL intensity. Because of the large radius of Hg(II), its affinity for N atoms, and its strong electron-withdrawing behavior [16,38,41], the TAPI-dots showed a preferential affinity for Hg(II) over Ag(I) at pH 6, which resulted in selective FL quenching. The zeta potential at pH 11 (Table S1) indicated the presence of a large number of negatively charged groups (primarily hydroxyl ions) on the surfaces of the TAPI-dots. The hydroxyl ions could act as bridges that link TAPI-dots together, which would result in the observed increases in the QY and particle size (Table S1). At the same time, the deprotonated surface would increase the affinity of the TAPI-dots for Ag(I) and contribute to the observed FL quenching. However, Hg(II) is easily hydrolyzed [the maximum concentration of Hg(II) is approximately 10−10 μM at pH 11), which would lower its concentration at high pH values. The TAPI-dots therefore showed selectivity for either Hg(II) or Ag(I), depending on the pH.

To verify the behavior of the TAPI-dots in real samples, quantification of Hg(II) and Ag(I) ions in water samples from Heaven Lake in the Celestial Mountains was attempted. This involved a standard addition method. The accuracy of the determination of known additions of Hg(II) and Ag(I) ions at four different concentrations was between 95% and 108%, with a relative standard deviation of 1.6–5.9%. These results show that the TAPI-dots are an effective, novel FL sensor for selectively monitoring Hg(II) and Ag(I) in real water samples (the results are listed in Table S2). A comparison of our results with those recently reported for FL detection of multiple metal ions by using carbon-based materials is given in Table 1 . The linear ranges and detection limits of the TAPI-dot FL sensor are lower than or comparable to those of the other sensors. Importantly, the ability to use the solution pH for metal ion selectivity has not been reported for carbon-based fluorescent probes.

In summary, we prepared novel TAPI-dots by an environmentally friendly, low-cost, and simple method. The prepared TAPI-dots gave a high QY and had good photostability. They were used as a label-free fluorescent probe for the selective detection of Hg(II) or Ag(I), with LODs of 11.4 and 10.3 nM, respectively. The detection of Hg(II) or Ag(I) in lake water was successful. Furthermore, tuning the pH to control the selectivity will enable the development of new, facile, FL-sensing probes with controllable selectivities.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21503271) and Shenzhen Peacock Plan (KQTD2015071616442225) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.07.090.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of heavy metal contaminations in urban soils, urban road dusts and agricultural soils from China
Interaction of humic acids with metal ions in the water medium
Recent advances in transition-metal dichalcogenides based electrochemical biosensors: a review
Individual and simultaneous electrochemical detection toward heavy metal ions based on L-cysteine modified mesoporous MnFe2O4 nanocrystal clusters
Comparative study of atomic fluorescence spectroscopy and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry for mercury and arsenic multispeciation

A magnetized nanoparticle based solid-phase extraction procedure followed by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry to determine arsenic, lead and cadmium in water, milk, Indian rice and red tea
Mercury in environmental samples: speciation, artifacts and validation
Use of a small molecule as an initiator for interchain staudinger reaction: a new ATP sensing platform using product fluorescence
Fluorescent CdSe QDs containing Bacillus licheniformis bioprobes for copper (II) detection in water
A polypeptide-mediated synthesis of green fluorescent gold nanoclusters for Fe3+ sensing and bioimaging
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Graphene quantum dots: recent progress in preparation and fluorescence sensing applications

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a Low-cost, Green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Fabrication of a nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot from MOF-derived porous carbon and its application for highly selective fluorescence detection of Fe3+
DNA derived fluorescent bio-dots for sensitive detection of mercury and silver ions in aqueous solution
One pot synthesis of highly fluorescent N doped C-dots and used as fluorescent probe detection for Hg2+ and Ag+ in aqueous solution
Preparation of cellulose-based fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their application in trace detection of Pb(II)
Chelation-enhanced fluorescence of phosphorus doped carbon nanodots for multi-ion detection
Formation of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon dots from biomass and their application for the selective detection of mercury and iron ion

One-pot green synthesis of high quantum yield oxygen-doped, nitrogen-rich, photoluminescent polymer carbon nanoribbons as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of silver(I) and mercury(II) ions
Carbon dots with continuously tunable full-color emission and their application in ratiometric pH sensing
Nanoreactor-confined synthesis and separation of yellow-luminescent graphene quantum dots with a recyclable SBA-15 template and their application for Fe(III) sensing
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Ammonium citrate derived carbon quantum dot as on-off-on fluorescent sensor for detection of chromium(VI) and sulfites
A general sensing strategy for detection of Fe3+ by using amino acid-modified graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probe
Highly fluorescent carbon dots as selective and visual probes for sensing copper ions in living cells via an electron transfer process
One-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots from ginkgo leaves and application in biology
Synthesis of cellulose-derived carbon dots using acidic ionic liquid as a catalyst and its application for detection of Hg2+
On–off–on fluorescent silicon nanoparticles for recognition of chromium(VI) and hydrogen sulfide based on the inner filter effect
One-pot green synthesis of oxygen-rich nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their potential application in pH-sensitive photoluminescence and detection of mercury(II) ions
One-step synthesis of orange luminescent carbon dots for Ag+ sensing and cell imaging
Rapid exfoliation of layered covalent triazine-based frameworks into N-doped quantum dots for the selective detection of Hg2+ ions
One pot synthesis of highly fluorescent N doped C-dots and used as fluorescent probe detection for Hg2+ and Ag+ in aqueous solution
Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers
Highly luminescent S-doped carbon dots for the selective detection of ammonia (vol 114, pg 544, 2017)
One step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphitic quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing strategy for highly sensitive detection of metacycline in mice plasma
A portable RGB sensing gadget for sensitive detection of Hg2+ using cysteamine-capped QDs as fluorescence probe
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells
The selectivity of the carboxylate groups terminated carbon dots switched by buffer solutions for the detection of multi-metal ions
Ions-induced two-photon fluorescence dual-switching for reversible and simultaneous sensing of Cu2+ and Hg2+ based on dual-emitting carbon dot/carbon dot conjugates

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Liang Luo was born in China in 1992. He obtained his BS from North China University of Science and Technology in 2015. Now he is a MS candidate in The Xinjiang Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His current research is focus on design and fabrication of fluorescence materials for imaging and sensing.

Fu Wang received his Ph.D. from Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2011. After his postdoctoral fellow working at Max-Planck-Institute for Biophysical Chemistry and University of Freiburg, he is now an Associate Professor at Shanghai Jiao Tong University. His research interests include development of advanced technical for optical/electrical imaging and sensing, and preparation of functional catalysts for renewable energy generation and conversion.
